caveats: a: Four-digit occupation data mix contract and proprietary-product mills.
a: The yarn-vision evidence includes a controlled laboratory prototype rather than scaled U.S. deployment.
a: Legacy textile equipment may already automate some exposed monitoring tasks through non-AI controls.
rho: The Census question measures any business-function use, not production implementation intensity.
rho: Fancy-yarn and general yarn research may not generalize across natural, synthetic, industrial, and recycled inputs.
rho: Small custom lots produce sparse, shifting training data.
e: No statistical source reports the contract-processing share of firms or revenue.
e: Many mills blend custom service and proprietary product business.
e: The frozen firm count uses a food-processing margin bridge that may materially misclassify textile firms.
s5: The strategic acquisition may exceed the target economic band.
s5: Small custom-wool examples may fall below it.
s5: Offered-for-sale status is not a completed qualifying transfer.
q: One public craft-scale price list may not represent industrial tolling contracts.
q: Customer concentration and technical differentiation can move retention in opposite directions.
q: The estimate excludes implementation cost, demand change, and volume effects.
d5: The BLS projection covers all textile mills at three digits, not NAICS 313110 contract work.
d5: Import declines in one period may reflect inventory correction rather than domestic substitution.
d5: Defense sourcing is durable but applies only to qualifying products and procurements.
o: No direct source measures operator displacement in contract yarn processing.
o: Operator requirement is distinct from labor intensity; automation can reduce workers while leaving an accountable mill necessary.
o: Imported standardized yarn can eliminate the outsourced U.S. service relationship even though a foreign operator remains.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Customers supply fiber or specifications and purchase recurring lots priced by processed or finished weight, setup, complexity, finishing options, design time, or negotiated industrial conversion terms.
lens.deviation_from_default: Narrowed to custom, toll, and contract processing because NAICS 313110 includes both repeat outsourced processing and economically distinct standardized product manufacturing.
lens.excluded_activities: Mills primarily selling standardized proprietary yarn as inventory, vertically integrated captive operations, hobby-only microprocessors below the economic band, distributors without processing, foreign plants, inactive facilities, and asset-only or non-control transactions.
lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market mills that repeatedly provide custom, toll, or contract fiber preparation, spinning, twisting, texturing, winding, blending, yarn design, or related processing to external industrial, apparel, craft, technical-textile, or government-supply-chain customers.

a.bridge: BLS measures the broader NAICS 313100 workforce, not eligible LMM contract processors, and yarn-AI studies measure technical quality-inspection capability rather than wage-weighted substitution. I map exposure to inspection, machine monitoring, maintenance diagnosis, scheduling, quoting, inventory, purchasing, and administrative work, while heavily discounting physical setup, doffing, material handling, cleaning, repair, and operator intervention. Academic prototypes may overstate real-plant exposure, while wage weighting of managers and engineers may partly offset the industry's large low-wage production share.
caveats: Four-digit occupation data mix contract and proprietary-product mills.
The yarn-vision evidence includes a controlled laboratory prototype rather than scaled U.S. deployment.
Legacy textile equipment may already automate some exposed monitoring tasks through non-AI controls.
a.change_evidence: Payroll-weighted task studies at 15-20 contract mills covering operators, inspectors, mechanics, supervisors, engineers, quoting, scheduling, inventory, and office work, with existing machine automation and sensor-based controls netted out.
a.rationale: BLS reports production occupations at 67.83% and maintenance at 12.22% of fiber, yarn, and thread-mill employment; textile winding, twisting, and drawing operators alone are 38.92%. Inspectors are 4.95%. Recent research demonstrates high-performing AI vision for fancy-yarn defects and measurements, while NIST identifies predictive quality, maintenance, scrap, throughput, and forecasting uses. Exposure is therefore meaningful but concentrated in monitoring and support tasks rather than most hands-on plant work.

d5.bridge: BLS projects real output for all textile mills, not contract fiber/yarn processors, and trade data measure imports rather than domestic service demand. Applying the broader textile-mill output growth rate produces a near-flat five-year anchor, then I widen it for outsourcing share, technical-textile mix, import substitution, apparel weakness, and customer insourcing. Berry Amendment demand protects some domestic yarn and fiber tiers, likely making the broad textile forecast understate protected niches, while global cost competition may make it overstate commodity contract work.
caveats: The BLS projection covers all textile mills at three digits, not NAICS 313110 contract work.
Import declines in one period may reflect inventory correction rather than domestic substitution.
Defense sourcing is durable but applies only to qualifying products and procurements.
d5.change_evidence: Customer-level constant-quality pounds processed and order backlog for eligible mills over five years, split by apparel, craft, defense, industrial, medical, automotive, recycled, and technical yarn, with lost bids classified by imports, insourcing, closure, and substitution.
d5.rationale: BLS projects textile-mill output rising from $19.4 billion in 2024 to $20.0 billion in 2034 in chained dollars, a 0.3% annual rate, while employment falls 9.5%. Commerce reported year-ending January 2024 yarn imports down 16.4%, reflecting volatility rather than a stable growth signal. The Berry Amendment requires domestic production for covered defense fibers and yarns. A near-flat base with a broad niche- and import-sensitive range is most defensible.

caveats: No statistical source reports the contract-processing share of firms or revenue.
Many mills blend custom service and proprietary product business.
The frozen firm count uses a food-processing margin bridge that may materially misclassify textile firms.
e.change_evidence: Classify Census/SUSB firms using establishment websites, customer-supplied-material terms, SIC/NAICS descriptions, and confidential revenue by contract-processing versus own-product sales; verify repeat external customer counts and LMM profitability.
e.rationale: Contract processing is clearly present: Barnet advertises contract synthetic-fiber and yarn processing, while Green Mountain Spinnery publishes a detailed custom-processing schedule. Yet NAICS 313110 also contains mills selling standard yarn and thread on their own account, and the public data do not separate service revenue from product sales. I therefore estimate that a minority, though not a trivial one, of LMM firms meet the recurring outsourced-service lens.

l.detail: BLS Industry Productivity (IPS) direct: labor compensation (L02) $1,008.7M / sectoral output (T30) $4,606.5M, year 2024

n.detail: margin 0.1525 (margins.json[31]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Food Processing EBITDA/Sales 15.25% (dominant subsector of NAICS 31)); revenue/employee $334,964 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 313110); class <5: 82 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,005,598 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $153,354 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 22 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,536,636 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $234,337 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 23 firms, avg revenue/firm $4,424,739 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $674,773 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 40 firms, avg revenue/firm $8,022,925 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,223,496 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 22 firms, avg revenue/firm $26,468,636 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $4,036,467 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 22 firms, avg revenue/firm $264,402,273 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $40,321,347 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 62

caveats: No direct source measures operator displacement in contract yarn processing.
Operator requirement is distinct from labor intensity; automation can reduce workers while leaving an accountable mill necessary.
Imported standardized yarn can eliminate the outsourced U.S. service relationship even though a foreign operator remains.
o.change_evidence: Customer churn interviews and mill order records identifying self-processing, standard-product substitution, offshore sourcing, consolidation to larger tollers, and products eliminated without replacement, plus evidence of fully autonomous spinning lines.
o.rationale: Year-five demand still entails converting physical fibers into yarn or thread through cleaning, carding, drawing, spinning, texturing, twisting, winding, inspection, and shipment. AI can monitor and optimize these steps but cannot deliver the physical product as software. Residual displacement comes from customers buying standardized imported yarn, vertically integrating, or moving volumes to larger automated plants rather than eliminating manufacturing altogether.

q.bridge: Green Mountain Spinnery publishes one small natural-fiber processor's pricing, not industrywide contract terms or realized benefit retention. Its setup fee, finished-pound price tiers, hourly sorting/design charges, per-pound surcharges, shipping pass-through, and deposit terms show how custom work is billed. I infer that efficiency in fixed per-pound work can be retained between price resets, while hourly work and transparent surcharges pass through more directly. Small-lot posted pricing may understate negotiated industrial customer power and overstate operator discretion.
caveats: One public craft-scale price list may not represent industrial tolling contracts.
Customer concentration and technical differentiation can move retention in opposite directions.
The estimate excludes implementation cost, demand change, and volume effects.
q.change_evidence: Review 30 customer agreements and quote histories across natural and synthetic contract processors for fee basis, raw-material pass-throughs, escalation, rebids, minimum lots, setup, expedite charges, quality credits, term, and post-productivity price changes.
q.rationale: Custom processing combines weight-based prices, setup and option fees, and some hourly work. AI savings on fixed per-pound jobs can initially accrue to the mill, but customers can compare quotes and volume tiers already share scale economies. Competitive renewal, open-book industrial contracts, and shorter lead times should transfer part of the benefit, while differentiated technical yarns and scarce domestic capacity support retention.

rho.bridge: Census reports adoption across all U.S. businesses, NIST gives general manufacturing practices, and yarn studies report technical feasibility, not the share of exposed labor implemented by LMM U.S. mills within five years. I raise implementation above current generic small-business adoption for well-defined vision and maintenance use cases, then reduce it for old equipment, variable fibers, small lots, weak data infrastructure, integration cost, and operator acceptance. Prototype performance likely biases the upper end upward.
caveats: The Census question measures any business-function use, not production implementation intensity.
Fancy-yarn and general yarn research may not generalize across natural, synthetic, industrial, and recycled inputs.
Small custom lots produce sparse, shifting training data.
rho.change_evidence: A survey of eligible mills recording machine age, controls and sensor coverage, inspection method, downtime and scrap data, software stack, capex plan, pilot results, vendor integration cost, and realized labor hours by workflow over five years.
rho.rationale: AI vision can detect yarn defects, thickness, and pattern irregularity, and the research literature describes a transition toward in-line monitoring. NIST recommends line-by-line pilots and stresses leadership, consistent data collection, and measurable pain points. Census shows AI adoption remains materially lower at very small firms. Five years is enough for selective quality, maintenance, and planning deployments, not wholesale automation of a fragmented legacy plant base.

s5.bridge: A 2025 acquisition of a U.S. specialty-yarn facility and two small custom-wool mill succession examples show transfer mechanisms but do not measure a qualifying five-year transfer rate for eligible LMM contract mills. One owner openly offers the business for sale after 17 years; another continued a mill through a 2018 ownership change. I translate these observations into moderate transfer probability, reducing for failed sales, closures, equipment-only transfers, and deals outside the LMM or contract lens. Public examples are selection-biased and likely overstate successful continuity.
caveats: The strategic acquisition may exceed the target economic band.
Small custom-wool examples may fall below it.
Offered-for-sale status is not a completed qualifying transfer.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year transaction and closure census for NAICS 313110 linking majority-control sales to operating continuity, buyer type, employee retention, real estate, equipment age, customer contracts, owner age, and succession plans.
s5.rationale: Specialty-yarn capacity can attract strategic buyers, and custom mills can be transferred to new owner-operators. The installed equipment, trained labor, customer backlog, technical recipes, and domestic qualification can support continuity, but obsolete machinery, environmental liabilities, energy costs, customer concentration, real estate, and dependence on individual craft knowledge can make a business nontransferable.

principal_driver: Machine-data, inspection, maintenance, and planning improvements applied to a labor-bearing physical process that customers still need performed.
principal_weakness: Most work is hands-on and already machine-mediated, while the eligible contract-processing population and its transfer rate are not statistically observed.
